titulo: The Last Bus from Hollow Creek
resumen: School bus number 9 returns at dusk for anyone still listening to the children lost in 1984.
contenido: In Hollow Creek, Kentucky, everyone knew school bus number 9 wasn't supposed to show up after six in the evening.
The problem was, it showed up anyway.

Nobody drove it. Nobody knew what depot it came from. It hadn't been listed in the school district's records since 1984, when a bus full of kids disappeared during a storm on the way to a basketball game.
Twelve students and one driver.
They never found the bus.
They never found the bodies.
All that turned up three days later was an orange basketball, bouncing by itself in the middle of the road.
Ever since then, every now and then, someone would claim they'd seen the yellow bus rolling through town at dusk. Old, filthy, with fogged-up windows and a red light blinking like a sick heart.
The adults said people were making things up.
The teenagers said something different.
They said that if the bus stopped in front of you and the doors opened, you could hear children laughing inside.
They also said that if you got on, you might find someone you had lost.
My best friend, Connor, lost his older sister when he was nine. She had been on the bus in '84.
He grew up hearing that his sister wasn't dead, just "still on route."
I thought that was a cruel thing to say.
Until the night Connor called me crying.
"It's in front of my house," he said.
"What is?"
On the other end of the line, I heard an old engine.
Then hydraulic doors opening.
And then a little girl's voice:
"Connor, hurry up. We saved you a seat."
Connor never answered again.
I went to his house, but the front door was open and his phone was lying in the yard.
There were wet tire tracks in the street, even though it hadn't rained.

The police said Connor had probably run away. Nobody in town wanted to talk about the bus. The older folks changed the subject. Teachers lowered their voices. The school principal said it was an urban legend and asked me "not to feed the panic."
So I did what Connor would have done for me.
I waited.
I sat at the old bus stop in front of the school gym, the same stop where the basketball team had gotten on in 1984. I had a flashlight, a pocketknife, and a photo of Connor with his sister.
At 6:06 p.m., the streetlights went out.
The sound came first.
A diesel engine, tired and breathing from inside the fog.
Then the bus appeared.
Old yellow paint, peeling and cracked, the number 9 painted in black. It stopped in front of me with a scream of brakes. The doors opened.
Inside, it smelled like hot vinyl, children's sweat, and old rain.
The driver had no face. Just a low cap and white hands on the steering wheel.
"Get on," a voice said from the back.
It was Connor.
I walked down the aisle.
The seats were full of children. Some wore clothes from the eighties. Others looked current. All of them stared straight ahead, still and silent, backpacks resting on their laps.
Connor was in the back seat beside a teenage girl in a basketball jacket.
His sister.
"I told you she was still here," he whispered.
But his smile wasn't his.
The girl looked at me sadly.
"You shouldn't have come."
The bus started moving.
Through the windows, Hollow Creek was gone. There was only a dark, endless road lined with wet trees. Rain was falling upward. Drops rose from the asphalt into the sky.
"Where are we going?" I asked.
The driver answered without turning around:
"To the game."
The children began singing a school song, slow and off-key.
Connor grabbed my arm.
"If we make it to the gym, we can stay. There's no pain here. No crying parents. No funerals. Just the game."
Then I saw the scoreboard above the windshield.
VISITORS: 0
HOME: 12
There were twelve points.
The twelve kids from '84.
And underneath, a new number blinked.
Connor.
Connor's sister squeezed my hand.
"Every new passenger gives the bus another loop," she whispered. "If nobody gets off, we keep going forever."
"How do I stop it?"
She looked at the driver.
"Make him remember the road."

I moved toward the front as the bus picked up speed. The children started screaming at me to sit down. The windows filled with small hands pounding from the outside, even though we were flying down the road.
I placed the photo of Connor and his sister beside the steering wheel.
The driver looked at it.
His hands began to shake.
For one second, his cap slipped back and I saw his face: an old man, soaked through, his eyes full of mud.
The driver from '84.
"I tried to get back," he murmured. "But the road wouldn't let me."
The bus jolted.
The lights went out.

The road vanished, and for an instant, we saw the truth: the bus was sunk beneath an old bridge, covered in mud, trapped for decades in a dry creek bed no one had searched because the map said that route didn't exist.
"Brake," I said.
The driver cried.
"I don't know how."
Connor's sister screamed:
"Now!"
I pulled the emergency lever.
The doors flew open into the darkness. Wind rushed in, full of voices. One by one, the kids from '84 began to stand. They weren't smiling anymore. They didn't look trapped anymore. They looked tired.
They jumped into the white light opening outside.
Connor wouldn't move.
"I can't leave her again," he said.
His sister hugged him.
"You're not leaving me. You're letting me go."
Then she pushed him toward me.
We fell together onto the pavement in front of the school, just as bus number 9 drove away wrapped in fog.
At sunrise, they found the real bus beneath the bridge at Miller's Creek.
Twelve bodies.
One driver.
The story was on every local channel. They called it an unsolved accident, a tragedy finally closed.
But Connor was never the same after that.
Sometimes, in the middle of class, he stares out the window like he can hear brakes.
And last week, in the school parking lot, someone found a basketball bouncing by itself.
On it, written in black marker, was a message:
"We need one more player."
